metrics.name: Wale
metrics.question: Will Matthew Wale be elected as the next Prime Minister of Solomon Islands following the May 2026 no-confidence vote?

simulation_result.question: Will Matthew Wale be elected as the next Prime Minister of Solomon Islands following the May 2026 no-confidence vote?
limitations: forecast decomposition — grounded prior 0.8333 (counted n=2); simulation-conditional 0.0 (resolved mass 0.01); headline 0.8333 via combiner_unavailable_range_only. Prior and simulation are reported separately and never blended by a fixed rule.
unresolved [unresolved_unknown_state]: 0.278 — explicit other_unknown_state mass; feasible-action bounds computed, never assigned prior/0.5
unresolved [unresolved_future_decision]: 0.714 — advance simulated time to the deadline; the obligation reopens the decision

no leakage-audited prior↔simulation reliability combiner is fitted — the headline is the simulation-conditional forecast; the grounded prior is reported separately; this is a stated reason Lean V2 must not become the default yet

blueprint.causal_thesis: Matthew Wale, as opposition leader, seeks to convert the no-confidence victory into his own premiership, but coalition fluidity and rival candidate John Agovaka create uncertainty. The 26-member coalition must vote; Wale's success depends on securing a majority among them and potentially attracting

validation_failures_final.what: action vote_for_pm requires authority ['mp status'] that none of its actors hold

pruned.reason: ceremonial: no discretion over any surviving terminal pathway

solomon_islands_coalition_instability.claim: Solomon Islands coalition governments are highly unstable, with frequent no-confidence motions and party switching.

provenance.definition: Solomon Islands coalition governments are highly unstable, with frequent no-confidence motions and party switching.
provenance.level_fallback_reason: all levels sparse (total 2 usable case(s)); rate is wide by construction

cases.description: In 2018, Prime Minister Rick Houenipwela survived a no-confidence motion but his coalition fractured shortly after.
cases.basis_quote: Prime Minister Rick Houenipwela survived a no-confidence vote on October 30, 2018, but his coalition government remained fragile.

cases.description: In 2019, Manasseh Sogavare was elected Prime Minister after a no-confidence vote ousted Houenipwela, but his coalition later faced defections.
cases.basis_quote: Manasseh Sogavare was elected Prime Minister on April 24, 2019, following a no-confidence vote, but his coalition experienced defections within months.

china_influence_pressure.claim: China exerts significant economic and diplomatic influence on Solomon Islands politics, often backing incumbents or preferred candidates.

provenance.definition: China exerts significant economic and diplomatic influence on Solomon Islands politics, often backing incumbents or preferred candidates.
provenance.level_fallback_reason: all levels sparse (total 1 usable case(s)); rate is wide by construction

cases.description: In 2019, China supported Manasseh Sogavare's government after he switched recognition from Taiwan to China.
cases.basis_quote: China provided substantial aid and infrastructure funding to Sogavare's government following the diplomatic switch in September 2019.

matthew_wale.quantity: Matthew Wale is elected Prime Minister

provenance.definition: Matthew Wale is formally elected by parliament and sworn in as Prime Minister.
provenance.inclusion_criteria: Wale receives majority vote in parliamentary election and is sworn in by Governor-General.
provenance.exclusion_criteria: Any other person is elected or no election occurs by deadline.
provenance.level_fallback_reason: all levels sparse (total 2 usable case(s)); rate is wide by construction

cases.description: In 2014, Matthew Wale was elected as Leader of the Opposition but never became Prime Minister despite multiple attempts.
cases.basis_quote: Matthew Wale was elected Leader of the Opposition on December 9, 2014, but did not become Prime Minister.

cases.description: In 2019, Wale contested the prime ministership after a no-confidence vote but lost to Manasseh Sogavare.
cases.basis_quote: Matthew Wale was a candidate for Prime Minister on April 24, 2019, but was defeated by Manasseh Sogavare in a parliamentary vote.

frederick_kologeto.quantity: Frederick Kologeto is elected Prime Minister

provenance.definition: Frederick Kologeto is formally elected by parliament and sworn in as Prime Minister.
provenance.inclusion_criteria: Kologeto receives majority vote in parliamentary election and is sworn in.
provenance.exclusion_criteria: Any other person is elected or no election occurs by deadline.
provenance.level_fallback_reason: all levels sparse (total 1 usable case(s)); rate is wide by construction

cases.description: In 2014, Frederick Kologeto served as a cabinet minister but never became Prime Minister.
cases.basis_quote: Frederick Kologeto was appointed Minister of Mines and Energy on November 17, 2014, but did not become Prime Minister.

outcome_reference_class.quantity: Matthew Wale is elected Prime Minister

provenance.definition: Matthew Wale is formally elected by the National Parliament and sworn in by the Governor-General as Prime Minister of Solomon Islands following the May 2026 no-confidence vote.
provenance.inclusion_criteria: Official announcement or credible news report that Wale received majority of votes in parliamentary election and was sworn in.
provenance.exclusion_criteria: Any other individual elected, or no election by August 1, 2026.
provenance.level_fallback_reason: all levels sparse (total 2 usable case(s)); rate is wide by construction

cases.description: In 2019, Manasseh Sogavare was elected Prime Minister after a no-confidence vote ousted Rick Houenipwela.
cases.basis_quote: Manasseh Sogavare was elected Prime Minister by parliament on April 24, 2019, after a no-confidence vote.

cases.description: In 2014, Manasseh Sogavare was elected Prime Minister after a general election, but no no-confidence vote preceded it.
cases.basis_quote: Manasseh Sogavare was elected Prime Minister on December 9, 2014, following the general election.

solomon_islands_national_parliament.quorum: Majority of members (26) must be present for vote to proceed
solomon_islands_national_parliament.consequence_of_nonparticipation: If no candidate receives majority by deadline, Governor-General may dissolve parliament and call new election.

weight_invariant.rule: every weight traces to a counted reference class or is explicit unknown mass; no qualitative label is mapped to a number

matthew_wale.claim: Matthew Wale has privately secured ironclad commitments from at least 26 MPs across the six-party opposition coalition, including key defectors from Manele's camp, ensuring his election as PM.
beliefs: Coalition discipline is stronger than historical norms
China will accept him as PM if he offers continuity on key infrastructure deals
goals: Consolidate coalition into a stable government
commitments: Secret pacts with party leaders on cabinet posts
Promise to review but not cancel Chinese-funded projects
matthew_wale.pressures: China signals willingness to work with him if he maintains economic ties

relationships.john_agovaka: rival but contained by party deal

historical_case_refs: Solomon Islands 2019 coalition shift where Sogavare lost then regained power
distinguishing_observations: Wale's legal persistence forced the vote
He is the long-term opposition leader
matthew_wale.action_if_state: Wale actively campaigns as the unity candidate, downplays factional differences, and prepares a transition team.

matthew_wale.claim: Wale's leadership is contested within the opposition coalition; several MPs from his own party and allied parties are secretly negotiating with Agovaka or even Manele's remnants, making his election uncertain.
beliefs: Agovaka is actively poaching his supporters
commitments: Offered deputy PM to key faction leaders
matthew_wale.pressures: China prefers a more pliable PM and may be backing Agovaka
historical_case_refs: 2007 Solomon Islands PM vote where Snyder Rini lost after initial lead
distinguishing_observations: Agovaka is explicitly named as 'most likely' by Lowy
Coalition size is exactly the minimum needed
matthew_wale.action_if_state: Wale scrambles to lock down votes, makes last-minute concessions, and may threaten to withdraw if not assured.
transition_triggers: Public defection of a key Wale ally

john_agovaka.claim: John Agovaka has assembled a cross-party coalition that includes not only opposition MPs but also defectors from Manele's camp and independents, giving him a clear majority of 27+ votes.
beliefs: China will back him as a stable partner
john_agovaka.pressures: China actively supports his candidacy through backchannels

relationships.jeremiah_manele: some of his MPs defected to Agovaka

historical_case_refs: 2014 Solomon Islands PM election where Manasseh Sogavare won with cross-party support
distinguishing_observations: Lowy Institute explicitly identifies him as 'most likely'
He is a lesser-known figure, potentially a compromise candidate
john_agovaka.action_if_state: Agovaka publicly presents himself as the unity candidate, highlights his cross-party appeal, and calls for a fresh start.

john_agovaka.claim: Agovaka cannot secure enough votes; his support is limited to a faction within the opposition, and Wale's camp remains solid, leaving him with fewer than 20 committed MPs.
beliefs: Wale has locked down the core opposition
He cannot overcome Wale's historical leadership
commitments: Offered to be deputy PM under Wale
john_agovaka.pressures: China may shift support to Wale if Agovaka appears weak
historical_case_refs: 2010 Solomon Islands PM vote where multiple candidates split the opposition
distinguishing_observations: Wale is the long-term opposition leader with name recognition
john_agovaka.action_if_state: Agovaka withdraws from the race and endorses Wale, seeking a unity government role.
transition_triggers: Public loss of a key supporter

frederick_kologeto.claim: Frederick Kologeto, as the mover of the no-confidence motion, positions himself as kingmaker and brokers a compromise candidate (possibly himself or a neutral figure) to avoid a divisive vote between Wale and Agovaka.
beliefs: Neither Wale nor Agovaka can command a stable majority
A compromise candidate is needed for stability
goals: Or secure a powerful role like Deputy PM or Speaker
commitments: Offered cabinet posts to both Wale and Agovaka camps
frederick_kologeto.pressures: China wants a predictable outcome; instability hurts investment
historical_case_refs: 2006 Solomon Islands PM election where a compromise candidate emerged after multiple ballots
frederick_kologeto.action_if_state: Kologeto calls for a unity government, proposes a third candidate, and uses his parliamentary bloc to enforce the compromise.
assumptions: His bloc is large enough to swing the vote
Both Wale and Agovaka accept compromise
transition_triggers: First ballot fails to produce a winner
China signals support for a compromise

frederick_kologeto.claim: Kologeto has a secret pre-existing alliance with Wale and delivers his party's votes to Wale, ensuring Wale's victory.
beliefs: Wale is the legitimate opposition leader
A Wale government will reward his party
goals: Secure key ministries for his party
commitments: Signed coalition agreement with Wale before the no-confidence vote
frederick_kologeto.pressures: China is neutral or leaning toward Wale
historical_case_refs: 2019 Solomon Islands coalition where Sogavare's party allied with a smaller party to secure majority
distinguishing_observations: They share a common goal of ousting Manele
frederick_kologeto.action_if_state: Kologeto publicly endorses Wale, delivers his party's votes, and expects a senior cabinet position.

jeremiah_manele.claim: Jeremiah Manele, despite being ousted, is secretly negotiating with opposition MPs to re-form a government under his leadership, possibly by exploiting divisions between Wale and Agovaka.
beliefs: He can win back enough defectors with promises of patronage
commitments: Offered ministerial posts to wavering opposition MPs
jeremiah_manele.pressures: China prefers continuity and may support his return

relationships.john_agovaka: potential ally if Agovaka betrays Wale

historical_case_refs: 2007 Solomon Islands where PM Snyder Rini lost a no-confidence vote but later returned as Deputy PM
jeremiah_manele.action_if_state: Manele secretly meets with disgruntled opposition MPs, offers incentives, and prepares to contest the PM vote if the opposition fails to unite.
assumptions: At least 4 opposition MPs can be flipped
transition_triggers: Public split between Wale and Agovaka

jeremiah_manele.claim: Manele concedes defeat and does not actively seek to return; he focuses on retaining his parliamentary seat and positioning for a future role.
historical_case_refs: 2014 Solomon Islands PM Gordon Darcy Lilo accepted defeat after losing no-confidence
distinguishing_observations: No immediate signs of a comeback effort
jeremiah_manele.action_if_state: Manele issues a statement congratulating the new PM and pledges a smooth transition.
transition_triggers: Wale or Agovaka wins on first ballot
China shifts support to new PM

vote_for_pm.description: MP casts vote for a candidate in the PM election.
vote_for_pm.validation: MP must be present and eligible.
